critic_raw:
CRITIQUE (Round 1):

1. [ISSUE-A] PANEL_SPECIFIC_VS_GENERIC_THRESHOLDING: Domain-calibrated threshold for startup model vs. generic PolyPhen-2 threshold. Threshold asymmetry.
2. [ISSUE-B] HOTSPOT_GENE_COMPOSITION_ADVANTAGE: 40% evaluation from two sarcomere hotspot genes. Classification easier there. Possible AUROC inflation.
3. [ISSUE-C] LIMITED_BASELINE_SET_FOR_MODERN_CLAIM: Only PolyPhen-2 compared. No modern deep-learning pathogenicity tools. Foundation-model claim unsupported.
defender_raw:
DEFENSE (Round 1):
[ISSUE-A] Domain calibration is appropriate for the intended application.
[ISSUE-B] Hotspot genes are clinically important and relevant.
[ISSUE-C] PolyPhen-2 is what clinicians use. Comparison against it is relevant.
adjudication_raw:
Round 1:
- [ISSUE-A]: OPEN — defender defends appropriateness of domain calibration for deployment but doesn't address that the AUROC comparison is unfair when thresholds are asymmetrically calibrated.
- [ISSUE-B]: OPEN — clinical importance of hotspot genes doesn't address whether overrepresentation in the benchmark inflates the reported AUROC.
- [ISSUE-C]: OPEN — clinical deployment of PolyPhen-2 doesn't support a scientific claim about 'foundation-model representations outperforming classical predictors.'

Round 2:
Critic [ISSUE-A]: For AUROC, threshold doesn't matter directly — AUROC is threshold-free. However, if the claim extends to any decision-threshold metric, the domain calibration point matters. On AUROC specifically, the threshold issue is less critical.
Defender [ISSUE-A]: Concede partial — AUROC is threshold-free, so Issue A is partially addressed for the AUROC comparison specifically. But for deployment decisions using the calibrated threshold, the asymmetry matters.
[RESOLVED: ISSUE-A — partially resolved; AUROC metric is threshold-free but calibrated threshold deployment claim remains]

Critic [ISSUE-B]: If 40% of the benchmark is concentrated in two genes with highly characterized hotspots, AUROC performance on those variants could be near-perfect and drive the overall AUROC. Reporting overall AUROC without stratum-specific breakdowns by gene is an evaluation completeness issue.
Defender [ISSUE-B]: Concede — performance by gene stratum should have been reported. Concentration in two hotspot genes may inflate AUROC without being representative of full-panel performance.
[RESOLVED: ISSUE-B — conceded]

Critic [ISSUE-C]: The claim 'modern foundation-model representations outperform classical variant-effect predictors' requires at least one modern comparator (AlphaMissense, ESM-1v, EVE, or similar). A 2002-era tool is not a suitable sole baseline for this claim.
Defender [ISSUE-C]: Concede — the paper's framing of foundation-model superiority cannot be supported with only PolyPhen-2 as the comparison. Including at least one modern deep-learning tool is necessary.
[RESOLVED: ISSUE-C — conceded]

All three resolved in critic's favor. Verdict: critique_wins.
